dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a priority 1 overdose at that business in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, can you tell me what happened?
He was found unconscious under a tree with inadequate breathing.
His eyes didn't open at first. He wasn't responding and his motor was absent.
Eyes were closed and didn't open to any initial stimulation.
No motor response at first. He was non-responsive.
Airway was not patent initially. Breathing was inadequate so interventions were required.
Breathing is inadequate, around 4 breaths per minute. He needs assisted ventilation.
I initiated BVM at 12 breaths per minute on arrival due to insufficient respirations.
Carotid pulse is strong, but respiratory effort remains minimal even after initial interventions.
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color. No signs of cyanosis observed.
We have a medical/surgical history of epilepsy, old MI, and stroke.
No current medications or known drug allergies reported.
After he came to, he admitted to snorting heroin earlier. His GCS started at 3 and improved to 15.
He is currently alert and oriented to 4/4 and has no verbal complaints.
His pulse started at 89 and improved to 85 later.
Respirations started at 18 but dropped to 12 after naloxone administration.
Initial BP was 158/88, later dropped to 135/84, and stabilized at 119/72.
SpO₂ began at 99 and remained mostly stable throughout transport.
EKG showed sinus rhythm throughout monitoring.
First glucose was 101; follow-up readings showed 0.
We gave 2mg of naloxone via intranasal to counter the opioid overdose.
IV attempts were performed four times in left and right arms, but all were unsuccessful.
ECG with 4 lead monitoring was implemented later. Rhythm remained sinus.
Patient is secured on the stretcher via five-point harness. Monitor settings adjusted for stable monitoring.
Rechecked pulse: 89 through 85. No significant change observed.
Respirations stayed at 12 during transport, better than pre-treatment.
Blood pressure improved from 158/88 to 119/72 by the end of transport.
Glucose was 101 at the beginning and remained non-detectable during follow-ups.
SpO2 remained stable at or near 99 through all observation periods.
Sinus rhythm maintained throughout all monitoring periods.
He had no pain at all. Pain score was 0 during repeated assessments.
Repeat history confirms no new developments-opioid-related, not medical in nature.
No active drug allergies; previous ones included hydrocodone and penicillin.
He has a history of seizures but no seizure activity was observed currently.
Medical protocol for opioid overdose, Protocol 7-1, was followed throughout.
Transporting to Chippenham Hospital's ED. The patient is alert and non-acute.
Overdose/Poisoning/Ingestion call-71 year-old female with known CVA, old MI, and epilepsy. Treated with Narcan.
Transported to Chippenham Bed 16 via stretcher with a full report to RN. Decontamination and restocking completed.
IV attempts unsuccessful. Naloxone effective; patient became alert and oriented to 4/4.
Glucose checked at 101 and 0-no IV but stabilized with standard protocol.
Opioid overdose protocol followed. No trauma found; drug ingestion confirmed via patient report.
Skin remained warm and intact. Pupils were pinpoint on arrival.
IV attempts per protocol at extremity veins in both arms-four unsuccessful attempts.
Initial GCS was 3; now he's alert and oriented to 4/4 with no verbal complaints.
Carotid pulse strong throughout. No change in rhythm. BP as expected with reversal.
No issues with EKG: sinus rhythm documented throughout transport.
Transport by stretcher due to non-emergent status with no complications.
Drug box exchange performed upon finalizing transport and updating equipment status.
Patient has no complaints and is now accepting transport, signs of opioid intoxication resolved.
Protocol followed appropriately, no deviation from protocol for patient care.
Eyes opened after naloxone administration.
He answered questions correctly upon awakening. Verbal responses were appropriate.
Patient followed commands and demonstrated use of all extremities post medication.
Airway improved after naloxone.
Bilateral breath sounds were noted post medication without wheeze or crackles.
Asymmetric chest movement initially corrected post naloxone.
Pulse remained strong with normal quality throughout the shift in BP.
Skin temperature appeared normal, no edema or redness observed during secondary survey.
Skin appeared dry, intact, no rashes or bite marks-that's consistent with initial.
No signs of trauma or burns noted during secondary skin inspection.
Pupils equal and reactive post Narcan. Eyes clear with no discharge or redness.
No signs of hemorrhage or obstruction in nose or mouth.
Heart sounds regular with no murmur during EKG or auscultation.
Breath sounds normal in bilateral lungs without added noise.
Abdomen soft and non-tender. No deformities or pulsations felt.
Full sensation and movement in all extremities.
Peripheral pulses present with good strength bilaterally.
SpO₂ remained within normal range during transport.
First reading was normal at 101; after, glucose became non-detectable.
Arterial pressure remained in healthy range and stabilized by transport end.
Respiratory effort improved from 4 to 12 and stayed stable.
Pulse within normal for age, rhythm smooth throughout vitals trend.
History confirmed opioid use. No new symptoms or ambiguous complaints.
Epilepsy part of history; recent no incident during rescuer observation.
No active drug allergies; historical but not interfering.
No pain noted; patient cooperative with transport and verbal response.
Patient fit into Protocol 7-1. No complications to necessitate other protocol.
Transported in stable non-acute status per protocol clearance.
Chippenham ED chosen for preliminary trajectory and fluency with care handoff.
Verified pulse and breaths throughout. IV attempts noted for documentation.
Stored monitoring and documentation complete. No leftover patient complaints.
No penetrating or blunt trauma signs found by secondary inspection.
Naloxone successfully reversed overdose signs. No lingering respiratory distress.
Patient secured and stable per bed 16 handoff. Full written report relayed.
Skin remains intact and warm. All prior records reviewed for alignment.
Episodes of breathing and movement confirmed via real-time monitoring.
Patient orientation consistent at all evaluations post-intervention.
Causative issue resolved by proper medication. Protocol remains in good review status.
Lifted and harnessed using 5-point method for transport to facility.
Chippenham remains the only needed site. No bed or facility detour needed.
From non-responsive to alert and oriented via medication-an intervention success.
No obstruction encountered, post-treatment airway is patent, otherwise.
No labored breathing, normal expansion and effort under vitals.
Blood pressure of 119/72; color clearing from pinprick pink to normal.
Previous strokes and MI per history-no aggravation observed since.
No signs of stroke re-activation or additional pathology concerns.
Patient remains conscious and stable, ready to be handed off.
No isolation or contagion risks. Non-bleeding, nothing in secondary observation.
No communicable signs. Virtual consent documented and patient verbal consent available.
Driver and nurse confirmed radiate alert status as transport begins.
Patient responded fully; no incapacitation at arrival. Emergency transfer status stands.
No changes to standard opioid reversal actions if re-effected.
Protocol contextual integrity matched with soundtrack. No pause or seek in field review.
Alert upon arrival, answers, oriented. Credited to Narcan application.
Physical responses to commands intact, motor skills unimpaired.
Spontaneous eye-opening observed.
Neck without stiffness. No signs of nuchal rigidity or tender lymph nodes.
Mentally alert with clarity of speech and improved short-term memory.
Teeth intact and spoke clearly.
No erosion or trauma to mouth.
鼻部检查中没有发现出血。
Chest movement symmetry confirmed at end of transport.
No distension, masses, or tenderness updated on repeat assessment.
Urine not observed in review.
Back and neck checked-no signs of abnormal curvature or soreness.
Final check at 99%-no hypoventilation, obstructive or otherwise.
Low post Narcan documented and non-actionable with no IV available.
Rhythm unchanged. Consistent sinus noted via monitoring.
Breathing consistent with ongoing monitoring throughout shift.
No reported active allergies in note. Only listed as prior anaphylactic triggers.
Epilepsy part of background-no new incidents during transport.
Confirmed adherence to Protocol 7-1. No redirection necessary for transport.
Secure harness verified. Transport coordination confirmed for Chippenham.
Took patient for prioritized evaluation at nearest receiving hospital.
All responses consistent with patient received for transport.
No signs of IV extravasation. None required for completion of ED report.
Patient ready for non-emergent entry from sidewalk to ED bed.
All actions matched per expected mnemonics and efficient per best practices.
Stretcher wheels locked and strap firmware tight for road movement.
All documentation signed out. No pending issues or unusual delays.
Protocol utilized without deviation and current backup updated at site.
Medication total of 2mg administration within recommended practice guidelines.
Confidential patient load per institutional review protocols for emergency.
